title: A Social Audit Tool: Citizen Report Card Survey
author: Pradhan, Dr. Bijay Lal
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
Abstract  
 Understanding the status of public services and the extent to which they meet the needs and expectations of citizens is essential for development planning. The Citizen Report Card (CRC) is a simple and commendable research method that systematically provides feedback to public agencies on both qualitative and quantitative aspects of their performance. It serves as an effective social audit tool, identifying causes of dissatisfaction, weak accountability, and areas requiring corrective action. 
 This paper outlines the process of conducting a CRC survey, highlighting six key steps for its effective implementation. A sample CRC study using a questionnaire survey was conducted in two Village Development Committees (VDCs) of Chitwan, Nepal. The study examined the satisfaction levels of citizens with the services received in the past six months. The findings indicate that most respondents rated the services as “okay,” suggesting either reluctance to respond critically or a tendency to avoid confrontation despite being service users.
steps_raw:
Abstract  
 Understanding the status of public services and the extent to which they meet the needs and expectations of citizens is essential for development planning. The Citizen Report Card (CRC) is a simple and commendable research method that systematically provides feedback to public agencies on both qualitative and quantitative aspects of their performance. It serves as an effective social audit tool, identifying causes of dissatisfaction, weak accountability, and areas requiring corrective action. 
 This paper outlines the process of conducting a CRC survey, highlighting six key steps for its effective implementation. A sample CRC study using a questionnaire survey was conducted in two Village Development Committees (VDCs) of Chitwan, Nepal. The study examined the satisfaction levels of citizens with the services received in the past six months. The findings indicate that most respondents rated the services as “okay,” suggesting either reluctance to respond critically or a tendency to avoid confrontation despite being service users.
